statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given an array $ a $ of length $ n $ . For an integer $ k $ , we call it piggy if and only if it is possible to sort $ a $ in non-descending order by performing the following operation an arbitrary number of times (possibly zero):

- First, select two indices $ i $ and $ j $ ( $ 1 \le i < j \le n $ ) such that $ |a_i - a_j| \ge k $ ;
- Then, swap $ a_i $ and $ a_j $ .

You need to determine the largest piggy integer $ k $ . If such an integer does not exist, output $ -1 $ .

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 2 \cdot 10^5 $ ) — the length of $ a $ .

The second line contains $ n $ integers $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n $ ( $ 1 \le a_i \le 10^9 $ ) — the elements of $ a $ .

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 2\cdot10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the largest piggy integer $ k $ .

If such an integer does not exist, output $ -1 $ instead.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
1
1
5
1 2 3 4 5
3
1 4 2
5
2 1 5 4 3
6
1 1 4 5 1 4
Output
-1
-1
2
2
3

Note
In the first and the second test case, no matter how large $ k $ is, you can always sort $ a $ in non-descending order by not performing any operations.

In the third test case, the largest piggy $ k $ is $ 2 $ . We can select $ i=2 $ and $ j=3 $ in the first operation, since $ |a_2-a_3|=|4-2|=2 \ge k $ , and $ a $ is sorted in non-descending order. It can be proven that no piggy integers larger than $ 2 $ exist.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: For any array of elements, if you can somehow swap any pair of elements (maybe indirectly), you can always sort the array.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: For some $$$i,j$$$ such that $$$|a_i-a_j| \lt k$$$, how to swap them with help from other elements?

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2187A - Restricted Sorting
Firstly, if $$$a$$$ is already sorted, then any integer $$$k$$$ is piggy, we can simply output $$$-1$$$.
Let $$$b$$$ be the array obtained by sorting $$$a$$$. For some $$$1 \le i \le n$$$, if $$$a_i=b_i$$$, we do not need to touch it. For $$$i$$$ with $$$a_i\ne b_i$$$, we need to swap them to their correct places. Let $$$mx=\max(a)$$$ and $$$mn=\min(a)$$$. Consider for an integer $$$k$$$, how do we check if it is piggy:
If $$$mx-mn \lt k$$$, we can swap none of the elements, so $$$k$$$ is not piggy.
If there exists some $$$i$$$ such that $$$a_i \ne b_i$$$ and $$$mx-a_i  \lt  k$$$ and $$$a_i - mn  \lt  k$$$, we can never swap this element, so $$$k$$$ is not piggy.
Otherwise, we claim that $$$k$$$ is piggy. This is because we can indirectly swap any pair of $$$a_i$$$ and $$$a_j$$$, with help from $$$mn$$$ and $$$mx$$$. Without loss of generality, consider two cases:
If $$$a_i-mn\ge k$$$ and $$$a_j-mn \ge k$$$, we can perform the following swaps (elements are shown by their relation positions):
$$$a_i$$$, $$$a_j$$$, $$$mn$$$
$$$a_i$$$, $$$mn$$$, $$$a_j$$$
$$$mn$$$, $$$a_i$$$, $$$a_j$$$
$$$a_j$$$, $$$a_i$$$, $$$mn$$$
If $$$a_i-mn \ge k$$$ and $$$mx-a_j \ge k$$$:
$$$a_i$$$, $$$a_j$$$, $$$mn$$$, $$$mx$$$
$$$mn$$$, $$$a_j$$$, $$$a_i$$$, $$$mx$$$
$$$mx$$$, $$$a_j$$$, $$$a_i$$$, $$$mn$$$
$$$a_j$$$, $$$mx$$$, $$$a_i$$$, $$$mn$$$
$$$a_j$$$, $$$mn$$$, $$$a_i$$$, $$$mx$$$
$$$a_j$$$, $$$a_i$$$, $$$mn$$$, $$$mx$$$
As shown in Hint 1, if we can swap any two elements, we can always sort the array.
With the above analysis, we can binary search for the largest piggy $$$k$$$, and check the answer in $$$O(n)$$$, with overall complexity $$$O(n \log n)$$$. In fact, we do not even need to perform binary search. The answer can be shown to be:
$$$$$$\min_{a_i \ne b_i}(\max(a_i-mn, mx-a_i))$$$$$$
